blocks.text: Molar mass (M) is the mass of one mole of a substance, measured in g/mol.
Where Ar is the relative atomic mass of the element (from the periodic table).
Amount of substance (n) is the number of moles; it relates mass to molar mass.

blocks.note: for gases, Vm = 22.4 L/mol at STP

blocks.label: Example:
blocks.content: How many moles in 49 g of H₂SO₄? → n = 49/98 = 0.5 mol

blocks.label: Example:
blocks.content: Mass of 3 mol of NaCl? → m = 3 × 58.5 = 175.5 g

blocks.text: Mass fraction of an element (ω) is the ratio of the element's mass to the total mass of the substance.
Where n is the number of atoms of the element in the formula.
Mass fraction of dissolved substance (ω) is the ratio of the solute mass to the solution mass.
On dilution: the mass of dissolved substance does not change, but the solution mass increases.

blocks.label: Example:
blocks.content: 20 g of salt in 200 g of solution → ω = 20/200 × 100% = 10%

blocks.label: Example:
blocks.content: 300 g of water added to 200 g of 10% solution → m(solute) = 20 g, m(solution) = 500 g → ω = 20/500 × 100% = 4%

blocks.text: Algorithm for calculations from a reaction equation:

items: Write the reaction equation and balance the coefficients.
Find the amount of substance (n) of the given substance: n = m / M.
Use the coefficients to find n of the desired substance.
Find the mass (or volume) of the desired substance: m = n × M.

blocks.label: Example:
blocks.content: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. How much CaO from 200 g CaCO₃? n(CaCO₃) = 200/100 = 2 mol → n(CaO) = 2 mol → m(CaO) = 2 × 56 = 112 g

blocks.text: Reaction yield (η) is the ratio of the actual mass of product obtained to the theoretically possible mass.
Theoretical mass is calculated from the reaction equation (η = 100%). In practice the yield is always less than 100% due to losses and side reactions.

blocks.label: Example:
blocks.content: Calculated: 112 g CaO; obtained: 89.6 g → η = 89.6/112 × 100% = 80%
